text: The surgeon uses a self-retaining retractor to expose the full thickness prolapse segment. Cautery is applied one centimeter proximal to the dentate line to circumscribe the rectal wall, followed by circumferential dissection to complete the rectotomy.

The surgeon enters the anterior peritoneal cavity and delivers the sigmoid colon transanally. The mesentery of the sigmoid colon is divided using a vessel sealing device, modifying the standard Altemeier operation approach.

The surgeon uses a GIA stapler to divide the sigmoid colon, then tags the staple line with a suture for future retrieval. A Babcock clamp is employed to deliver the sigmoid colon back into the abdominopelvic cavity.
A TAMUS port access channel is positioned and secured to the dermis at the one and seven o'clock positions. The TAMUS port lid is clamped onto the access channel, and insufflation is initiated.
The surgeon divides adhesions and explores the abdomen, similar to a diagnostic laparoscopy. The omentum is visibly adherent to the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon sweeps the sigmoid conduit to the patient's left to expose the sacral promontory. Using a laparoscopic tacker, the sigmoid epiploics are secured to the sacral promontory with three absorbable vicryl tacks, preparing for the rectopexy.

Endocyanine green is administered to assess bowel wall perfusion before removing the TAMIS port. The tagged sigmoid colon is delivered towards the anorectum, and the staple line is excised. A hand-sewn coloanal anastomosis is performed using interrupted 2-0 vicryl sutures. Endocyanine green is used again to confirm conduit perfusion, and the completed anastomosis is displayed.

Synthetic mesh is applied to reinforce the repair and PEXI. Following the rectotomy, the mesentery is divided, and the sigmoid conduit is stapled. The staple line is then tagged with a suture for retrieval.

The surgeon positions the tamus port and establishes pneumopelvis with the patient in Trendelenburg position. The sigmoid conduit is grasped and delivered back into the abdominal cavity, exposing the posterior aspect of the pelvis, which is vacant following the excision of the rectum and mesorectum. A straight six-centimeter section of polypropylene mesh is delivered through the tamus port and tacked into place using absorbable tacks.

The surgeon removes the tamis port and delivers the tagged staple line of the sigmoid conduit to the operative field. The distal portion of the polypropylene mesh is divided in the middle to form two pant legs. These mesh pant legs are wrapped around the sigmoid colon conduit and secured at the three and nine o'clock positions using 3-0 PDS sutures. The anastomosis is then completed with a hand-sewn technique.
